questions.question: Why is the prescribed dose of dialysis based on removal of urea rather than on urea's own toxicity?

options.A: Urea is only slightly toxic itself, but its plasma level reflects concentrations of other, presumably more harmful uremic toxins
options.B: Urea is the single most toxic uremic solute
options.C: Urea is a large middle molecule that is difficult to clear
options.D: Urea has no relationship to any other uremic toxin

questions.explanation: Urea is chosen as the dose marker because it is only slightly toxic per se; its plasma level instead reflects the concentrations of other, presumably more harmful, small uremic toxins, and it is convenient to measure (MW 60 Da). B is wrong: urea is not the single most toxic solute. C is wrong: urea is a small solute, not a middle molecule. D is wrong: urea levels do track other toxin levels. Small toxins appear more important than large ones for uremic toxicity, so dialysis dose is prescribed on urea removal via URR and Kt/V.

questions.question: What is the approximate molecular weight of urea, the marker solute used to gauge dialysis dose?
questions.explanation: Urea's molecular weight is 60 Da, making it a small, easily dialyzed solute that is simple to measure, so it serves as the standard marker for quantifying dialysis dose. 113 Da is nearer creatinine, and roughly 11,800 Da approximates beta-2 microglobulin, a middle molecule; 500 Da is far too high. Because uremic toxicity stems mostly from small solutes and urea is both small and readily measured, removal of urea via URR or Kt/V gauges adequacy even though urea itself is only slightly toxic.

questions.question: Why can a low serum urea level be misleading when assessing dialysis adequacy?

options.A: It always confirms that dialysis is adequate
options.B: Serum level depends only on the rate of removal
options.C: A low level may occur when removal is poor but urea generation is also low, such as with poor protein intake
options.D: Serum urea has no relationship to protein intake

questions.explanation: A low serum urea can mislead because the serum level depends on both the rate of removal and the rate of generation. A patient with poor removal but low urea generation from poor protein intake can still show a low level, so C is correct. A is wrong: a low level does not confirm adequacy. B is wrong: level depends on generation too, not removal alone. D is wrong: urea generation is tightly linked to protein intake. Monitoring urea removal matters more than the serum number; if removal is inadequate, dialysis is inadequate regardless of the level.

questions.question: The urea generation rate is closely linked to which of the following?

options.D: The protein nitrogen appearance rate, because most protein nitrogen is excreted as urea

questions.explanation: The urea generation rate is linked to the protein nitrogen appearance rate because most protein nitrogen is excreted as urea, so generation tracks dietary protein and catabolism; thus D is correct. Dialysate flow, membrane surface area, and patient height (A, B, C) affect clearance or volume estimates but not the generation of urea itself. This linkage explains why a low serum urea can reflect low protein intake and low generation rather than good clearance, and why the nutritional marker nPNA is derived directly from urea kinetics.

questions.question: In the National Cooperative Dialysis Study, treatment failure increased dramatically when the thrice-weekly spKt/V fell below which value?
questions.explanation: In the National Cooperative Dialysis Study, thrice-weekly treatment failure rose dramatically when spKt/V fell below 0.8 compared with values above 1.0, so A is correct. 1.2 and 1.4 are the later KDOQI minimum and target, not the failure threshold, and 1.7 was a HEMO study dose. Large observational studies gave similar results. This landmark finding underpins the recommended minimum spKt/V of 1.2 with a target of at least 1.4, deliberately set as a safety margin well above the 0.8 failure zone.

questions.question: For thrice-weekly hemodialysis, what minimum and target spKt/V do the KDOQI Adequacy Workgroups recommend?
questions.explanation: The KDOQI Adequacy Workgroups recommend a minimum spKt/V of 1.2 with a target of at least 1.4 for thrice-weekly dialysis, so B is correct. A (0.8/1.0) reflects the NCDS failure zone, not the recommendation; 1.4/1.7 and 1.7/2.1 overstate the requirement. The target of 1.4 is set above the 1.2 minimum to provide a margin, since the delivered dose varies from month to month owing to lab and technique factors. This minimum corresponds to a weekly standardized Kt/V (stdKt/V) of about 2.1.

questions.question: A thrice-weekly spKt/V of 1.2 corresponds to approximately what weekly standard Kt/V (stdKt/V) when volume contraction is taken into account?
questions.explanation: A thrice-weekly spKt/V of 1.2 translates to a weekly stdKt/V of about 2.1 when calculated by modeling or a method accounting for volume contraction, so C is correct. 1.4 is the spKt/V target, 1.7 was a HEMO high-dose arm, and 3.7 was the average stdKt/V in the FHN Daily Trial. The stdKt/V of 2.1 is the anchor used to set minimum doses across all dialysis frequencies, chosen precisely because it equals a standard three-times-weekly spKt/V of 1.2.

questions.question: The European Best Practice Guidelines define their minimum amount of dialysis as which of the following?
questions.explanation: The European Best Practice Guidelines define their minimum dose as an eKt/V of 1.2, which is slightly higher than the KDOQI spKt/V-based minimum because eKt/V runs about 0.15 units below spKt/V; thus D is correct. A minimum spKt/V of 1.2 (A) is the KDOQI single-pool standard, not the European equilibrated value, while URR 65% (B) and stdKt/V 1.7 (C) are not the stated European minimum. Using the equilibrated value accounts for postdialysis urea rebound, effectively demanding somewhat more delivered dialysis.

questions.question: Equilibrated Kt/V (eKt/V) values tend to be about how much lower than single-pool Kt/V (spKt/V)?
questions.explanation: Equilibrated Kt/V is typically about 0.15 units lower than single-pool Kt/V, with the exact gap depending on the rate or intensity of dialysis, so A is correct. The difference arises from postdialysis urea rebound as urea re-equilibrates between body compartments. 0.05 is too small, and 0.5 or 1.0 units grossly overstate the gap. This is why the European eKt/V minimum of 1.2 is effectively a higher demand than the same single-pool value, and why rebound is larger in smaller, more rapidly dialyzed patients.

questions.question: In the HEMO study, an spKt/V of 1.7 was compared with an spKt/V of 1.3. What was the overall result for the higher-dose group?

options.A: They lived longer and were hospitalized less
options.B: They did not live longer, were not hospitalized less, and showed no nutritional benefit
options.C: They had markedly better nutritional status only
options.D: The trial could not be interpreted

questions.explanation: In the HEMO study an spKt/V of 1.7 versus 1.3 (doses actually defined by eKt/V) showed the higher-dose group did not live longer, was not hospitalized less, and gained no nutritional or other measured benefit, so B is correct. A and C claim benefits that were not observed, and D is wrong because the trial was interpretable and clearly negative overall. HEMO and the NCDS are the only large randomized adequacy trials, so most dose recommendations above the proven minimum remain largely opinion-based.

questions.question: In the HEMO trial subgroup analysis, which group appeared to benefit from the higher dialysis dose?
questions.explanation: In the HEMO subgroup analysis, women assigned to the higher dose survived longer, while men did slightly worse, making the overall trial effect negative; thus C is correct. A (men) and B (both sexes) contradict the data, and D is wrong because a difference was seen. Whether this dose-gender interaction is real or a statistical fluke is unclear. One proposed rationale is that scaling dose to body surface area rather than to urea volume V would give women relatively more dialysis, since their V-to-BSA ratio is lower.

questions.question: The ratio of urea distribution volume (V) to body surface area (BSA) differs between men and women by roughly what amount, prompting the argument that women may need more dialysis?
questions.explanation: The V-to-BSA ratio differs by about 12%-15% between men and women, so if both have equal V they receive equal Kt/V, yet the woman's BSA is 12%-15% higher, arguing she needs roughly 15% more dialysis; hence D. 2%-3% is far too small, and 25%-30% actually refers to how much higher the minimum spKt/V in women might need to be, since raising stdKt/V requires roughly twice the spKt/V increase. In health, GFR naturally scales to BSA, which underpins the argument for BSA-based dosing.

questions.question: Why may short session lengths that achieve an adequate Kt/V in small patients still be problematic?

options.A: They may not allow adequate removal of middle molecules or excess fluid, risking chronic overhydration

questions.explanation: Short sessions can hit a target Kt/V in small patients yet fail to remove middle molecules and excess fluid adequately, risking chronic overhydration, so A is correct. B is backwards, since short sessions clear fewer, not more, middle molecules, and C and D (reuse, hyperkalemia) are not the concern raised. Additionally, short sessions in patients with large interdialytic weight gains force high ultrafiltration rates, which are linked to poor outcomes. This is a key reason to set targets on both Kt/V and dialysis time.

questions.question: In one large study, when patient urine volume exceeded 100 mL per day, what was the effect of delivered dialysis dose on survival?

options.B: Dose had little impact on survival

questions.explanation: In one large study (Termorshuizen, 2004), when patient urine volume exceeded 100 mL per day the amount of dialysis delivered had little impact on survival, so B is correct. A and C overstate a dose effect that was not seen, and D is wrong because survival was assessed. This suggests residual kidney function is protective and may permit lower dialysis doses, though methods for adjusting dose for residual function remain entirely opinion-based, with suggested guidance available from the European Best Practice and NKF-KDOQI documents.

questions.question: In the FHN Daily Trial that showed a benefit of more frequent dialysis, what was the approximate average delivered stdKt/V?
questions.explanation: The FHN Daily Trial, which showed a benefit of more frequent dialysis, delivered an average stdKt/V of about 3.7, far above the 2.1 minimum, with roughly 5 treatments per week and an average session length of 154 minutes; thus C is correct. 2.1 is the minimum standard dose, 2.7 is not cited, and 5.0 confuses the number of weekly treatments with the dose. The trial illustrates that frequent schedules deliver much higher weekly standardized clearance than conventional thrice-weekly dialysis.

questions.question: The predialysis serum albumin should be measured every 3 months; at what level does increased mortality risk begin?
questions.explanation: Increased mortality risk begins at a predialysis serum albumin below 4.0 g/dL (40 g/L), so D is correct; levels below 3.0 g/dL (30 g/L) mark high risk of morbid events and warrant a search for the cause. Values of 2.5 or 3.5 g/dL are not the stated inflection point. Serum albumin, measured every 3 months, is an important indicator of nutritional state and a very strong predictor of subsequent illness or death in dialysis patients, which is why the risk threshold sits at the relatively high level of 4.0 g/dL.

questions.question: According to kinetic modeling using a standard Kt/V approach, twice-weekly dialysis is generally inappropriate for which patients?

options.A: Patients without some modest degree of residual kidney function
options.B: Patients with substantial residual kidney function
options.C: All incident patients regardless of urine output

questions.explanation: Kinetic modeling using a standard Kt/V approach suggests twice-weekly dialysis is not appropriate for patients without at least some modest residual kidney function, so A is correct. B is the opposite, since residual function is exactly what makes twice-weekly tenable; C is wrong because urine output matters; and D is not the criterion. Twice-weekly dialysis is common in the developing world for economic reasons, and preliminary data suggest starting incident patients twice weekly may preserve residual kidney function longer, with one US observational study showing no clear harm.

questions.question: For solutes such as phosphorus and middle molecules, what is the major determinant of their removal?
questions.explanation: Total weekly dialysis time is the major determinant of removal for phosphorus and middle molecules, so B is correct. Blood flow rate and dialyzer K0A (A, C) mainly drive small-solute urea clearance, and dialysate temperature (D) is unrelated to solute mass removal. Short weekly time also makes it difficult to remove salt and water safely and effectively. This is why urea Kt/V alone is an incomplete adequacy measure, and why minimum session lengths are recommended alongside Kt/V targets to ensure adequate clearance of larger solutes.

questions.question: The US KDOQI 2006 adequacy work group recommended what minimum session length for patients dialyzed three times weekly with little residual renal function?
questions.explanation: The US KDOQI 2006 adequacy work group recommended a minimum session length of 3 hours for patients dialyzed three times weekly with little residual renal function, so C is correct. 2 or 2.5 hours fall below the recommendation, and 4.25 hours is the time being tested in the TiMe trial, not the KDOQI minimum. Longer time aids removal of phosphorus, middle molecules, and excess fluid; the European Best Practices Group recommends an even higher minimum of 4 hours.

questions.question: The European Best Practices Group recommends a minimum treatment time of how many hours?
questions.explanation: The European Best Practices Group (2002) recommends a minimum 4-hour treatment time, so D is correct. 3 hours is the US KDOQI minimum, and benefits beyond 3.5 hours are unclear and hard to demonstrate in the United States (though greatest in Japan and intermediate in Europe); 5 hours is not the stated European minimum. Longer time improves removal of middle molecules and fluid. The US average dialysis time is about 3.5 hours and rising toward 4, similar to practice in the rest of the world.

questions.question: The TiMe trial underway in the United States tests whether setting a minimum dialysis time of what value for all incident patients improves outcomes?
questions.explanation: The TiMe trial tests whether setting a minimum dialysis time of 4.25 hours for all new (incident) patients, regardless of body size, yields meaningful outcome benefits, so A is correct. 3.0 hours is the KDOQI minimum and 3.5 hours the current US average, while 6 hours reflects in-center nocturnal dialysis. The trial addresses whether guaranteeing longer time, independent of Kt/V-based dosing that can be met quickly in small patients, improves survival by allowing better fluid and middle-molecule removal.

questions.question: A dialysis prescription centers on two main components. What are they?
questions.explanation: A dialysis prescription centers on two components: K, the dialyzer clearance (which depends on dialyzer size and blood flow rate), and t, the session length; their product K x t defines the dose, so B is correct. Temperature and pH, sodium and bicarbonate composition, and URR or nPNA (A, C, D) are separate orders or monitored outputs, not the core dose determinants. Dividing K x t by the patient's urea distribution volume V yields Kt/V, the central adequacy metric of the prescription.

questions.question: For adult patients dialyzed at a blood flow of 400 mL/min, dialyzer clearance K usually is about which value?
questions.explanation: For adult patients dialyzed at a blood flow of 400 mL/min, dialyzer urea clearance K is usually about 230 plus or minus 30 mL/min, with an overall range of roughly 200-260 mL/min, so C is correct. 100 mL/min is too low, while 400 and 500 mL/min confuse clearance with the blood or dialysate flow rates. Actual K depends on the dialyzer K0A and the flows and can be read from a nomogram; combined with session length t, this clearance determines the delivered K x t and thus Kt/V.

questions.question: If clearance K is 250 mL/min and session length is 4 hours, what is the total K x t (volume of blood cleared of urea)?
questions.explanation: With K = 250 mL/min over a 4-hour (240-minute) session, K x t = 250 x 240 = 60,000 mL, or 60 L, the total volume of blood cleared of urea, so D is correct. 30, 45, and 100 L result from arithmetic errors. Dividing this K x t by the patient's estimated urea volume V gives Kt/V; for instance, a prescribed Kt/V of 1.4 would require a V of about 43 L (about 78 kg), illustrating how body size limits the dose achievable within a fixed session length.

questions.question: KDOQI guidance suggests prescribing a Kt/V of about what value to ensure the delivered dose stays above 1.2?
questions.explanation: KDOQI guidance suggests prescribing a (K x t)/V of about 1.4 to ensure the delivered dose stays above the 1.2 minimum, so A is correct, because month-to-month variability in lab values and technique can pull the delivered value down. 1.2 is the minimum itself and leaves no margin; 1.7 was a HEMO study dose and 2.1 is the weekly stdKt/V. Building this buffer between the prescribed and the minimum delivered dose protects patients from inadvertent underdialysis on any given treatment.

questions.question: The best method to estimate a patient's urea distribution volume V for prescribing uses which approach?

options.B: Anthropometric equations incorporating height, weight, age, and gender, such as Watson's

questions.explanation: The urea distribution volume V is best estimated from anthropometric equations incorporating height, weight, age, and gender, such as Watson's (with the Hume-Weyers equations as an alternative), so B is correct. Serum creatinine (A) reflects muscle mass, BSA alone (C) is a different scaling metric, and a flat weight times 0.42 (D) ignores age and gender. Accurate V is essential because the required K x t is obtained by multiplying V by the desired Kt/V; for African American patients, 2 kg is added to the Watson value.

questions.question: When estimating V using the Watson equation for an African American patient, what adjustment is recommended?

options.C: Add 2 kg to the Watson value

questions.explanation: When estimating V with the Watson equation for an African American patient, add 2 kg to the Watson value (Vant), so C is correct. Subtracting 2 kg (A), adding a fixed 5 L (B), or making no adjustment (D) are incorrect. This upward correction improves the accuracy of the urea distribution volume estimate, which then feeds the calculation of required K x t (V multiplied by the desired Kt/V). Alternatively, the Hume-Weyers equations or a nomogram derived from them can be used to estimate V.

questions.question: To achieve a K of 250 mL/min at a blood flow of 400 mL/min, approximately what minimum dialyzer K0A is required per the nomogram?
questions.explanation: To achieve a K of 250 mL/min at a blood flow of 400 mL/min, the nomogram (Figure 13.6) places you on a K0A line of about 900, so a dialyzer with a K0A of at least 900 mL/min is needed; hence D. 500 and 700 are too low to deliver that clearance, while 1,400 exceeds what is required. If such a high-efficiency dialyzer is unavailable, one must dialyze longer than 4 hours; increasing dialysate flow to 800 mL/min adds only about 5%-10% to clearance.

questions.question: Increasing dialysate flow from 500 to 800 mL/min, when blood flow exceeds 400 mL/min, typically increases clearance by approximately what amount?
questions.explanation: Raising dialysate flow from 500 to 800 mL/min, when blood flow exceeds 400 mL/min, typically increases clearance by about 5%-10%, so A is correct; it does not produce no change, nor a 25%-30% or 50% jump. With some modern dialyzers that use spacer yarns to optimize dialysate distribution around the fibers, going from 600 to 800 mL/min was shown to add very little (Ward, 2011). The optimum dialysate flow is generally 1.5 to 2.0 times the blood flow rate.

questions.question: The optimum dialysis solution flow rate is generally what multiple of the blood flow rate?
questions.explanation: The optimum dialysis solution flow rate is 1.5 to 2.0 times the blood flow rate, so B is correct. A flow of 0.5 times would under-supply fresh dialysate and limit the diffusion gradient; 3-4 times gives little added benefit for the cost; and exactly 1.0 times is suboptimal. The standard dialysate flow is 500 mL/min, and increasing it to 800 mL/min raises clearance only about 5%-10% (and even less with dialyzers whose dialysate path is already optimized), consistent with this 1.5-2.0 times guidance.

questions.question: To achieve a URR of 70%, what Kt/V is needed when no fluid is removed versus when weight loss is 6% of body weight?
questions.explanation: To reach a 70% URR you need a Kt/V of only 1.3 when no fluid is removed, but 1.5 when weight loss (UF/W) is 6% of body weight, so C is correct. A reverses the relationship, B ignores the effect of fluid removal, and D overstates it. Ultrafiltration adds convective urea removal but also concentrates blood, so larger fluid losses raise the Kt/V needed to achieve a given URR. Patients with large interdialytic weight gains therefore require a higher prescribed Kt/V to hit the same URR target.

questions.question: According to KDOQI guidelines, how often is the dialysis dose usually monitored by drawing pre- and postdialysis serum urea nitrogen?
questions.explanation: KDOQI guidelines call for monitoring the dialysis dose monthly by drawing paired pre- and postdialysis serum urea nitrogen to compute the URR and spKt/V, so D is correct. Weekly (A) is more often than recommended, every treatment (B) applies to machine-based dialyzer sodium conductivity or UV dialysate absorbance clearance, and every 3 months (C) matches serum albumin, not urea dosing. Averaging three monthly spKt/V values can help confirm the 1.2 minimum is being met given month-to-month variability.

questions.question: Why must a properly drawn (slow-flow or stop-dialysate) postdialysis blood specimen be used when computing URR?

options.A: Because access recirculation can falsely lower the postdialysis value via admixture with dialyzer outlet blood
options.B: To prevent hemolysis of the sample
options.C: To keep the specimen warm before analysis
options.D: Because urea rebounds instantly to predialysis levels

questions.explanation: A properly drawn postdialysis specimen is essential because access recirculation can falsely lower the postdialysis urea through admixture with dialyzer outlet blood, spuriously raising the URR and Kt/V; hence A. It is not about hemolysis (B), keeping the specimen warm (C), or instant rebound to predialysis levels (D). A slow-blood-flow or stop-dialysate-flow technique clears recirculated blood from the arterial line before sampling, giving a true postdialysis SUN and an accurate delivered dose.

questions.question: In the KDOQI slow-flow method for drawing the postdialysis SUN, the blood pump is slowed to what rate before the sample is drawn?
questions.explanation: In the KDOQI slow-flow method the blood pump is slowed to 100 mL/min for 10-20 seconds and then stopped before the sample is drawn, so B is correct; the other rates and durations are incorrect. The UF rate is first set to zero. Because access recirculation reverses quickly, the inflow urea concentration rises within about 10-20 seconds once flow is slowed (depending on roughly 10 mL of arterial-line dead space), so this brief slow-flow period clears recirculated blood and yields an accurate postdialysis SUN.

questions.question: In the second-generation Daugirdas equation for spKt/V, what does the term R represent?

options.C: The postdialysis SUN divided by predialysis SUN (i.e., 1 minus URR)

questions.explanation: In the second-generation Daugirdas equation, spKt/V = -ln(R - 0.008 x t) + (4 - 3.5 x R) x UF/W, the term R is (1 - URR), equal to post-SUN divided by pre-SUN, so C is correct. It is not residual clearance (A), the UF rate (B), or dialyzer clearance (D). In the formula, t is the session length in hours, UF is the weight loss in kilograms, and W is the postdialysis weight. This equation is a KDOQI-approved alternative to full urea kinetic modeling programs such as Solute Solver.

questions.question: Dialysis patients have the lowest mortality risk at which predialysis serum potassium range?
questions.explanation: Dialysis patients have the lowest mortality risk at a predialysis serum potassium of 5.0-5.5 mmol/L, so D is correct; the risk rises greatly for values over 6.5 and under 4.0 mmol/L. The lower ranges in A and B and the high range in C are not the nadir. This U-shaped relationship informs dialysate potassium choices: survival is highest against a 3 K bath or higher, whereas chronic use of 1.0 mM potassium dialysate has been associated with increased cardiac arrest (Lafrance, 2006).

questions.question: To increase Kt/V from 1.4 to 1.5 (a 7% increase), what is the simplest adjustment applied to a 4-hour treatment?

options.A: Add about 17 minutes of session time

questions.explanation: The simplest way to raise Kt/V from 1.4 to 1.5 (a 7% increase) is to lengthen the session by 7%, adding about 17 minutes to a 240-minute treatment (1.07 x 240 = 257 minutes); hence A. Doubling blood flow (B) is usually not achievable and would overshoot, halving dialysate flow (C) lowers clearance, and switching to a low-flux dialyzer (D) reduces efficiency. Alternatively, the K term can be raised via a higher blood flow, a larger dialyzer, or an 800 mL/min dialysate flow (about a 5%-10% gain).

questions.question: A sudden rise in a patient's modeled V, with an unchanged prescription, most commonly indicates what?

options.A: The patient's true urea volume markedly increased
options.B: Less dialysis was actually given than was ordered or anticipated

questions.explanation: A sudden rise in modeled V with an unchanged prescription almost always means less dialysis was actually delivered (a reduced K or t) than ordered, so B is correct. True urea volume rarely changes abruptly (A), the dialyzer does not spontaneously become more efficient (C), and urea generation does not stop (D). The modeling computer, knowing only that Kt/V fell while K x t was supposedly constant, can explain it solely by inferring a larger V, thereby flagging problems such as access recirculation, a shortened run, or a reduced blood flow.

questions.question: Patients in whom reaching an spKt/V of at least 1.2 is difficult fall into which three categories?

options.C: Poor access, very large patients, and those with frequent hypotension or side effects
options.D: New starts, transplant candidates, and travelers

questions.explanation: The three categories of patients in whom reaching an spKt/V of at least 1.2 is difficult are those with poor access (limiting blood flow or causing recirculation), very large patients, and those with frequent hypotension, angina, or other side effects that force blood-flow reductions; hence C. The groupings in A, B, and D are not the ones the chapter describes. Recognizing these categories guides practical solutions such as improving the access, lengthening dialysis time, or moving to a four-times-per-week schedule.

questions.question: With a four-per-week schedule and residual renal urea clearance below 2.0 mL/min per 1.73 m2, KDOQI 2006 suggests the minimum spKt/V can be reduced to about what value?
questions.explanation: With a four-per-week schedule and residual renal urea clearance below 2.0 mL/min per 1.73 m2, KDOQI 2006 allows the minimum spKt/V to fall from 1.2 to about 0.8, so D is correct. 0.5 is too low, and 1.2 and 1.4 are the standard thrice-weekly minimum and target. More frequent dialysis raises weekly standardized clearance, permitting a lower per-session dose while still meeting a stdKt/V near 2.1, and it also avoids the higher-risk long weekend interdialytic interval.

questions.question: One advantage of a four-times-per-week schedule is that it avoids which higher-risk period?

options.C: The long weekend interdialytic interval, around which adverse events and deaths are more common
options.D: The first hour of each dialysis session

questions.explanation: A four-times-per-week schedule avoids the long weekend interdialytic interval, around which adverse events and deaths are more common (Foley, 2011), so C is correct. It is not about the midweek treatment (A), the shift timing (B), or the first hour of dialysis (D). Beyond eliminating the dangerous 3-day gap, four sessions per week help larger and hypertensive patients and those hard to ultrafilter, and they permit a reduced per-session minimum spKt/V (about 0.8) when residual clearance is below 2.0 mL/min per 1.73 m2.

questions.question: In the HEMO trial, significant survival benefit from high-flux membranes was seen in which predefined subgroup?

options.B: Patients on dialysis longer than 3.7 years

questions.explanation: In the HEMO trial, significant survival benefit from high-flux membranes appeared in the predefined subgroup of patients on dialysis longer than 3.7 years (the patient median), so B is correct; the overall 8% survival gain did not reach statistical significance. Diabetics, patients under 50, and women (A, C, D) were not the defined benefiting subgroup. Cardiovascular mortality appeared reduced across all high-flux patients, a finding generally in accord with the European MPO trial (Locatelli, 2009).

questions.question: Both KDOQI and the European Best Practices Group recommend using a high-flux membrane under what condition?

options.B: Only when dialyzer reuse is practiced
options.C: Where proper water treatment is available
options.D: Only for patients on dialysis less than one year

questions.explanation: Both the KDOQI Adequacy Workgroup and the European Best Practices Group recommend high-flux membranes where proper water treatment is available, so C is correct; the recommendation is not limited to diabetics (A), reuse programs (B), or patients on dialysis under a year (D). Adequate water purity matters with these more permeable membranes. High-flux use may also lower the incidence of beta-2 microglobulin amyloidosis in long-term patients, whether through enhanced clearance or less procedure-related inflammation.

questions.question: The so-called dry weight (optimum postdialysis weight) is best defined as which of the following?

options.B: The lowest weight the patient can tolerate before fainting
options.C: The ideal body weight for the patient's height
options.D: The postdialysis weight at which all or most excess body fluid has been removed

questions.explanation: Dry weight, better termed the optimum postdialysis weight, is the postdialysis weight at which all or most excess body fluid has been removed, so D is correct. It is not the predialysis weight minus a fixed 2 kg (A), the lowest weight before fainting (B), or the ideal body weight for height (C). Set too high, the patient stays fluid-overloaded and risks edema or pulmonary congestion; set too low, they suffer hypotension. It is found by trial and error, allowing for the roughly 0.2 L of saline given during blood return.

questions.question: Malaise, a washed-out feeling, cramps, and dizziness after dialysis suggest which problem with the postdialysis weight?

options.A: The dry weight is set too low (patient ultrafiltered below optimum)
options.B: The dry weight is set too high
options.C: The dialysate sodium is too high
options.D: The dialysate calcium is too low

questions.explanation: Malaise, a washed-out feeling, cramps, and dizziness after dialysis indicate the patient was ultrafiltered below the optimum postdialysis weight, i.e., the dry weight is set too low, so A is correct. A dry weight set too high (B) instead leaves fluid overload with edema or dyspnea. High dialysate sodium (C) causes thirst, and low dialysate calcium (D) relates to cardiac arrest risk, not this syndrome. The postdialysis recovery in over-ultrafiltered patients is described as extremely stressful and unpleasant, prompting reassessment of the target weight.

questions.question: How often should the optimum postdialysis weight be reevaluated to avoid overhydration if a patient loses flesh weight?
questions.explanation: The optimum postdialysis weight should be reevaluated at least every 2 weeks, so B is correct, because if a patient loses flesh weight the old dry weight becomes too high and, if maintained, causes overhydration and hospitalization for fluid overload. Yearly (A) or 6-monthly (C) reassessment is too infrequent, and waiting for edema (D) is unreliable since clinical signs miss occult overload. A progressive fall in the optimum postdialysis weight can itself be a clue to an underlying nutritional disturbance or disease process.

questions.question: Evidence suggests patients have higher survival when the ultrafiltration rate is kept below what threshold?
questions.explanation: Evidence indicates higher survival when the ultrafiltration rate is kept below 12 mL/kg per hour (Movilli, 2007), so C is correct. 5 mL/kg/hr is unnecessarily restrictive, while 20 and 30 mL/kg/hr are above the protective threshold; high UF rates are associated with poor outcomes. The rate can be lowered by extending dialysis time, but reducing interdialytic weight gain through sodium restriction is often more practical and acceptable, and diuretics help lower UF needs in patients with substantial residual urine volume.

questions.question: What is the standard dialysis solution flow rate?
questions.explanation: The standard dialysis solution flow rate is 500 mL/min, so D is correct. 300 mL/min is below standard, while 800 mL/min is the higher rate sometimes used to gain about 5%-10% more clearance when blood flow is high and a high-K0A dialyzer is used, and 1,000 mL/min is not standard. The optimum dialysate flow is generally 1.5 to 2.0 times the blood flow rate, so 500 mL/min pairs well with typical blood flows in the 300-400 mL/min range.

questions.question: Which base is the fluid of choice for dialysis solution, with the alternative now considered obsolete in most countries?
questions.explanation: Bicarbonate is the base of choice for dialysis solution, and acetate-based dialysate is now considered obsolete in most countries, so A is correct; lactate and citrate (C, D) are not the standard maintenance bases. Concentrate typically supplies bicarbonate around 32 mM plus a small amount of acetate. The base concentration is titrated to a predialysis plasma bicarbonate of 20-23 mmol/L, keeping in mind that sodium diacetate (Granuflo) concentrates can add as much as 8 mM of extra bicarbonate-generating base to the dialysate.

questions.question: The base concentration should be adjusted to achieve a predialysis plasma bicarbonate concentration in what range?
questions.explanation: The base concentration should be adjusted to achieve a predialysis plasma bicarbonate of 20-23 mmol/L, so B is correct; 15-18 is too acidic, while 24-28 and 28-32 risk overcorrection. A definite clinical benefit of pushing predialysis HCO3 above 20-23 has not been shown, and doing so may cause postdialysis metabolic alkalosis with a theoretical increased risk of calcium-phosphorus precipitation and cardiac arrhythmia. Consistently, monitored predialysis serum bicarbonate has its lowest mortality between 20 and 22.5 mmol/L.

questions.question: What is the usual dialysis solution potassium level, and when is it typically raised to 3.0 mM?

options.A: 4.0 mM usually; raised only for hyperkalemia
options.C: 2.0 mM usually; raised to 3.0 mM if predialysis plasma K is below 4.5 or the patient takes digitalis

questions.explanation: The usual dialysis solution potassium level is 2.0 mM, raised to 3.0 mM when the predialysis plasma potassium is below 4.5 mmol/L or the patient is receiving digitalis, so C is correct. A (4.0 mM), B (1.0 mM routine), and D (3.5 mM, never adjusted) are wrong. Chronic use of 1.0 mM potassium dialysate has been linked to increased cardiac arrest (Lafrance, 2006), and survival is highest against a 3 K bath or higher (Jadoul, 2012); malnourished patients with low predialysis potassium also need a higher bath to avoid hypokalemia.

questions.question: What is the usual dialysis solution sodium range, and what is associated with levels above 138 mM?

options.B: 150-155 mM; above 138 mM lowers blood pressure
options.C: 138 mM fixed; higher levels prevent thirst
options.D: 135-145 mM; above 138 mM is associated with increased thirst and interdialytic weight gain

questions.explanation: The usual dialysis solution sodium range is 135-145 mM (typically 138 mM); levels above 138 mM are associated with increased thirst and interdialytic weight gain, so D is correct. A (120-130 causing cramps), B (150-155 lowering BP), and C (fixed 138 preventing thirst) misstate the values. Higher sodium may raise blood pressure, though the extra fluid can often be removed with fewer symptoms, whereas levels below 135 mM predispose to hypotension and cramps. One study suggests patients may have individual sodium set points.

questions.question: Dialysis solution calcium levels in chronic patients normally range within which values?
questions.explanation: Chronic dialysate calcium normally ranges 1.25-1.5 mM (2.5-3.0 mEq/L), so A is correct; 0.5-1.0, 2.0-2.5, and 3.0-3.5 mM are outside the usual range. The usual level with calcium-containing phosphate binders is 1.25 mM (2.5 mEq/L), adjusted up or down per clinical response and parathyroid hormone status. Patients on calcium-free resin binders may need a higher bath to avoid negative calcium balance, while dialysate calcium below 1.25 mM has been associated with increased risk of sudden cardiac arrest (Pun, 2013).

questions.question: What is the usual dialysis solution magnesium level, and what is the current trend?

options.B: 0.25-0.5 mM, with a trend toward using 0.5 mM at the higher end
options.C: 1.0-1.5 mM, with a trend toward lower levels

questions.explanation: The usual dialysis solution magnesium level is 0.25-0.5 mM (0.5-1.0 mEq/L), with the trend toward 0.5 mM at the higher end, so B is correct; 2.0, 1.0-1.5, and 0.1-0.2 mM are incorrect. Dialysis patient survival is generally better when patients are not hypomagnesemic, favoring the higher end. Frequent use of proton-pump inhibitors in this population may reduce oral magnesium absorption and increase the risk of hypomagnesemia (Alhosaini, 2014), which is linked to atrial fibrillation and poor cardiovascular outcomes.

questions.question: The dialysate temperature should be set as low as tolerated without discomfort, generally within which range?
questions.explanation: Dialysate temperature should be set as low as tolerated without discomfort, generally 34.5-36.5 degrees C, so C is correct; 30-32, 37-38, and 38.5-40 degrees C are outside that range. Cooler dialysate protects against intradialytic hypotension and shortens postdialysis recovery time. Individualizing it by measuring tympanic temperature and setting dialysate 0.5 degrees C lower retains these benefits while avoiding shivering, and may reduce myocardial stunning; chronic cool dialysis was linked to better cardiovascular survival (Hsu, 2012).
